duration: About 4 minutes, including a 2-minute period while I couldn’t find it. So about 2 minutes total.
specific_location: Greenlee Preserve. First, in a cottonwood on the northeast corner of Waneka Lake. Then in a dead tree at the southwest corner of Greenlee Preserve (which is also the northwest corner of Waneka Lake). There are two dead trees there, and the bird was in the tree more toward the west (you can see these on GoogleMaps).
description: Note that the first part of this description is based on my 20-second observation at a distance of 175-200 feet. The bird was clearly a meadowlark: plump, short-tailed, and with a forward-leaning posture. It had a bright yellow breast. I tried to observe the bird’s face, and I could not discern any yellow on the bird’s malar. That is consistent with Eastern Meadowlark. However, at a distance of up to 200 feet (with 10x binoculars), I was probably not able to fully assess the exact color of the malar; still, the absence of yellow is consistent with Eastern Meadowlark. Just as important (and I think this is an under-appreciated field mark for Eastern vs. Western Meadowlark), I was struck by the bird’s pale-headed look overall--an effect contributed to mainly by the pale auriculars. The pale auriculars are most prominent on the Eastern subspecies lilianae, but they are also relatively pale on nominate magna.  The next part of this description is based on observation of the bird in flight. As it prepared to flush, I had closed into within 125–150 feet. As the bird flew away, I was struck by the extent of white on the bird’s outer rectrices; the bird seemed whiter than normal for a Western Meadowlark. Observation of the bird in flight was brief: about 2 seconds.Behaviors: The bird was vocal and restless.Call: Note that a sound recording and a sound spectrogram are included in this submission. When I first heard the bird's song, I recognized it as the primary song of the Eastern Meadowlark: clear, sweet (unmodulated), down-slurred whistles. I briefly considered the possibility that the bird was a Red-winged Blackbird. A common call of the Red-winged Blackbird is a clear, downslurred whistle--occasionally given in series. Then I began to approach the bird again, and it got flighty. I saw the bird as it was preparing to flush, but I could hear no flight call as it flushed this second time, and the recorder does not seem to have picked up a flight call, either.Plumage: Adult
